params.text:
Objects of reference

An object of reference is a real object used to stand for an activity, person, or place. Showing someone their coat can mean “we are going out”; a cup can mean “would you like a drink?”. Objects are concrete and easy to understand, which makes them valuable for people who find pictures or words difficult, including some people with a learning disability or a sensory impairment.

  • Consistent: the same object should always mean the same thing.
  • Meaningful: choose objects that connect naturally to the activity.
  • Multi-sensory: an object can be seen, touched, and sometimes heard or smelled.

Giving people time

One of the most powerful things you can offer is time. Many people need longer to process what you have said and to form a response — this is sometimes called processing time. Rushing a person, or answering for them, takes away their voice and can leave them anxious and unheard.

Ask one thing at a time, then pause and wait. Resist the urge to fill the silence or to guess. Give the person space to point, sign, reach for an object, or find their words. A few extra seconds of patience often makes the difference between a person communicating for themselves and being spoken for.

params.question: A person you support takes a long time to respond to questions. What is the most person-centred approach?

answers.text: Ask one question at a time, then pause and give them plenty of time to respond in their own way.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Giving processing time and asking one thing at a time lets the person communicate for themselves.

answers.text: Answer for them to save time and keep things moving.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Answering for someone takes away their voice and can leave them unheard.

answers.text: Ask several questions quickly so they only need to respond once.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Multiple questions at once are harder to process. Ask one at a time and wait.

answers.text: Assume no response means they have nothing to say and move on.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. A slow response is not no response. Give the person time and watch how they reply.
